one_line_takeaway: A design-science study showing that a decomposed, prompt-engineered GPT-4 pipeline can auto-generate automotive functional-safety HARA (safety goals) rated useful and human-comparable by expert reviewers, cutting HARA time from months to under a day while still requiring human review — a functional-safety RE contribution, not an AI-security one.
key_finding: LLMs can generate HARA safety requirements that experts judged useful and comparable to human-written results, but only when HARA is decomposed into guided sub-tasks with definitions, detailed process descriptions, and few-shot examples in the prompts; an unconfigured/general LLM lacks the domain-specific knowledge and produces incorrect results (notably for the novel CAEM function vs. the familiar AEB). Experts scored highest on criteria 'g' (safety goals formulated for events above severity S0 — a rule-based Python step, not the LLM) and 'd' (safety mechanisms excluded), and on 'i' (safety-goal quality/unambiguity); they disagreed most on 'b' and 'c' (identifying and correctly formulating relevant hazardous events, tied to the difficulty of arguing completeness). No criterion averaged below 2. The tool completed a HARA in under a day vs. months for a human team. Experts concluded LLMs are a valuable preliminary/brainstorming aid but cannot yet replace the human process; human Verification and Confirmation Reviews remain essential. Hallucination and unknown/uncontrollable training data were the dominant limitations (RQ1).
core_contribution: The paper designs and evaluates an LLM-based prototype that automates Hazard Analysis and Risk Assessment (HARA) to specify functional-safety requirements (safety goals) for autonomous-driving (AD) functions. Its contribution is an automated pipeline of prompts and GPT models that takes only an item definition (function description) as input and outputs a human-readable HARA table of safety goals, plus a redundancy/contradiction finder step. HARA is decomposed into LLM-manageable sub-tasks, some of which (e.g., methodically combining scenarios with malfunctions, and calling the API for every hazardous event with severity > S0) are deliberately handled by rule-based Python scripts rather than the LLM to avoid hallucination where neither creativity nor NL understanding is needed. A second contribution is four explicit safety-goal derivation strategies (avoid failure mode, avoid exposure/ODD restriction, improve controllability, reduce severity). This is a functional-safety (ISO 26262 / ISO 21448 SOTIF) requirements-engineering paper, NOT an adversarial AI-security paper.
threat_model: Not applicable. This is a functional-safety and requirements-engineering study; there is no adversary, attack surface, or threat model in the security sense. The 'safety' addressed is automotive functional safety and safety of the intended functionality (SOTIF), i.e., avoiding hazardous behavior from malfunctions/functional insufficiencies of the AD function, not defending an AI system against malicious actors. The paper explicitly notes (External Validity) that its patterns could be adapted to cybersecurity/Threat Analysis & Risk Assessment (TARA), but that is future/adjacent work, not what this paper does.
limitations: Evidence is predominantly qualitative expert judgment on a single novel function (CAEM), with small samples (9 experts in cycle 2, 4 participants in cycle 3) and no quantitative accuracy/recall metrics against ground truth. Only a clustered subset of generated safety requirements could be reviewed (the LLM itself selected diverse samples), so completeness of hazardous-event identification could not be systematically established — indeed the experts noted no accepted systematic completeness method exists. Different expert cohorts across cycles (a deliberate anti-bias choice) hinder cross-cycle comparison. Results are tied to specific GPT models/versions and may not generalize. Hallucination and the inability to interpret figures/dense diagrams (requiring plain-text inputs) are acknowledged. The authors state the prototype was used only for the study and not in any production engineering.

datasets_used: Item definition and HARA for Autonomous Emergency Braking (AEB) — well-documented function used to test LLM prior knowledge
Item definition and HARA for Collision Avoidance by Evasive Maneuver (CAEM) — novel function, less mature/less public, primary evaluation target in engineering cycle 1
A novel case-company automotive function (item definition not publicly available) with an existing internal HARA baseline, used in engineering cycle 2 / case study
ISO 26262-derived review checklist (criteria a-j) and ISO 26262 / ISO 21448 (SOTIF) standards as process references; PEGASUS scenario factors referenced
Study artifacts released on Zenodo (protocol 10649245; sample LLM tests 10644052; sample LLM-based HARA 10522786)
quantitative_results: 9 automotive safety experts evaluated the CAEM HARA in engineering cycle 1; from 3 different AD development companies; average >10 years relevant experience (minimum 5 years)
71 review comments received from the 9 experts covering 20 hazardous events; comments clustered into 5 major root-cause categories
Expert scores on 5-option scale (1=not fulfilled in all rows ... 5=fulfilled in all rows; option 3 'No opinion' excluded and range rescaled 1-4 for averaging)

Highest average scores on criteria 'g' (safety goals for events with severity > S0; a rule-based Python step) and 'd' (safety mechanisms excluded); criterion 'i' (safety-goal quality/unambiguity) also fulfilled
Lowest/most-contested scores on criteria 'b' and 'c' (identifying and correctly formulating relevant hazardous events); no average score fell below 2
Case study (engineering cycle 2): 4 case-company team members, individual sessions lasting 1-2 hours each
4 safety-goal derivation strategies defined (avoid failure mode; avoid exposure via ODD; improve controllability; reduce severity)
Time efficiency: full HARA generated by the tool in less than one day vs. the months typically required by a team of human experts
baselines_compared: AEB (familiar, publicly documented function) vs. CAEM (novel function) to expose the effect of training-data familiarity on LLM HARA quality
LLM-generated CAEM HARA reviewed blind against expert judgment via ISO 26262 Verification Review (VR) and Confirmation Review (CR)
In engineering cycle 2, the LLM-generated HARA of the novel case-company function compared against the case company's existing internal (human-engineered) HARA baseline
